channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Why Xi Doesn’t Need a Deal With Trump
item.description: President Xi Jinping of China is buying time for Beijing and may see an opening with a U.S. president weakened by the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: President Xi Jinping of China, seen here in Beijing on Tuesday, has been seeking to redefine the terms of how China and the United States should engage.

item.title: China’s State Dinner Menu for Trump Includes Beef Ribs, Roast Duck and Tiramisu
item.description: A mixture of Chinese and international dishes were on the menu as Beijing’s chefs sought to appeal to President Trump’s tastes.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado
item.media:description: President Trump during the State Banquet on Thursday.

item.title: China’s Growing Leverage Over the U.S., and How Oil Companies Hit the Jackpot
item.description: Plus, a surprise reversal in the Murdaugh murder case.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas, Ian Stewart, Patricia Cohen and Colby Smith
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with Xi Jinping, China’s leader, on Thursday in Beijing.

item.title: Inside Jack Schlossberg’s Chaotic Campaign to Revive Camelot
item.description: Erratic behavior and staff turnover have colored Mr. Schlossberg’s bid for a House seat in New York, raising questions about his readiness for office.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:description: Jack Schlossberg, a Kennedy heir who is running to represent much of Manhattan in Congress, has churned through campaign staff at an unusual pace.

item.title: South Carolina Governor Plans Special Session to Redraw House Maps
item.description: Gov. Henry McMaster, a Republican, appears prepared to thrust the state into the nation’s redistricting wars.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:description: Gov. Henry McMaster’s decision to call back the state legislature came a day after the State Senate voted to block a resolution to do so.

item.title: In Qatar, Energy Sector Damage Is Severe, and the Way Back Will Be Long
item.description: Iranian strikes and a blockade have paralyzed Qatar’s gas engine, creating a technical bottleneck likely to stall exports for years.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A liquefied natural gas production facility in Qatar’s Ras Laffan Industrial City. Qatar is one of the world’s largest exporters of L.N.G., but Iranian attacks have crippled its production.

item.title: Gen. Caine’s Silence on Iran War Leaves Questions About Military Strategy
item.description: The president’s top military adviser is walking a tightrope as he leads the military through a divisive and unpopular war.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: In public, Gen. Dan Caine has avoided discussing with lawmakers the broader U.S. military strategy.

item.title: Iran War Powers Vote Fails Again in Senate, but GOP Opposition Grows
item.description: Senator Lisa Murkowski of Alaska became the latest Republican to switch her vote to halt the conflict and require President Trump to win congressional approval to continue it.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Lisa Murkowski, Republican of Alaska, during a committee hearing. She previously opposed similar resolutions.

item.title: Trump’s Remark on Americans’ Finances and Iran War Points to GOP Midterm Challenges
item.description: Asked if he was motivated by Americans’ financial woes to make a deal to end the war with Iran, he responded, “Not even a little bit.”
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: How the Stars of 6 Tony-Nominated Broadway Shows Get Into Character
item.description: For Nathan Lane, Taraji P. Henson, Daniel Radcliffe and other actors, it can take wigs, group hugs or banishing ghosts to make the transformation.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson, George Etheredge, Amir Hamja and Vincent Tullo
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: Ishmael Reed Is Writing a Play About Elon Musk
item.description: Ishmael Reed, provocateur and playwright, has a few words for the billionaires of Silicon Valley.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld
item.media:credit: Christie Hemm Klok for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ishmael Reed, 88, an author who has frequently challenged American political culture, at his home in Oakland, Calif. He is writing a play about Elon Musk and other tech billionaires.

item.title: Trump Administration Will Withhold $1.3 Billion in Medicaid Payments to California
item.description: Vice President JD Vance said the state had not done enough to fight fraud in the public health insurance program.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance said that California had failed to combat fraud in Medicaid.

item.title: Justice Dept. Sues D.C. Bar Over Efforts to Discipline Government Lawyers
item.description: The move escalates the administration’s feud with legal disciplinary bodies over the ethical conduct of government lawyers.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, accused the D.C. Bar, which handles complaints of lawyer misconduct, of acting as a “blatantly partisan arm of leftist causes.”

item.title: Duffy’s ‘Great American Road Trip’ Prompts Ethical Concerns
item.description: A YouTube series starring Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and his family is part of the nation’s 250th anniversary celebrations, but it doubled as a family excursion for them.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.category: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Duffy has insisted that the department’s career ethics and budget officials reviewed and approved his participation in the YouTube series.

item.title: Wes Streeting Resigns, Setting Stage for Possible UK Leadership Challenge to Starmer
item.description: After days of speculation, the British health secretary said Thursday that he was resigning from the government of Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Wes Streeting walking on Downing Street, London, where he had a meeting with Prime Minister Keir Starmer, on Wednesday.

item.title: Honda Posts First Ever Annual Loss After Pullback From E.V.s
item.description: The Japanese automaker posted its first loss since 1957 as it took a multibillion-dollar hit from scaling back its electric-vehicle plans.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:description: A Honda dealership in Osaka, Japan.

item.title: Crypto Industry Pushes a Bill to Tilt Regulation in Its Favor
item.description: After a series of political victories under President Trump, firms are lobbying Congress for a sweeping framework they helped shape.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany

item.title: Cecilia Flores Became the Voice of Mexican Mothers
item.description: One of the most prominent activists for Mexico’s disappeared recently found the remains of one missing son. Now she has turned her attention to finding the other.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner and Fred Ramos
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cecilia Flores during a search for her missing son, Alejandro Guadalupe Islas Flores, in Juan José Ríos, Sinaloa, Mexico.

item.title: Life, Death and Rebirth in Nepal, the Land of the Buddha
item.description: Starting at the birthplace of Buddhism, a writer traces how its teachings spread across Asia, transforming the continent forever.
item.dc:creator: Aatish Taseer and Keerthana Kunnath

item.title: In Thailand, Buddhism Is Twinned With Political Power
item.description: In Thailand, where it’s the state-sanctioned religion, practices have proved to be surprisingly mutable.
item.dc:creator: Aatish Taseer and Tomoko Yoneda

item.title: How Taiwan Became a Refuge for Buddhism
item.description: A Sinicized form of the religion has been preserved on the island, where daily life itself now sometimes seems like an exercise in Buddhist practice.
item.dc:creator: Aatish Taseer and Maxime Fossat

item.title: Argentina Races To Find Origin of Hantavirus Outbreak
item.description: The scientific investigation into the origin of cruise infection has become entangled with international finger pointing.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola
item.media:description: A sign shows information on hantavirus at a hospital in Bariloche, Argentina. A patient with no known connection to the cruise ship is hospitalized there with hantavirus.

item.title: In a City of Big Dreams, Many Young Adults See a Cloudy Future
item.description: A bleak job market. Rising rents. Huge debt. In New York and other cities, traditional milestones of adulthood feel further away for some 20- and 30-year-olds.
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson
item.media:credit: Tessa Belle Dillman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Soban Ali moved to New York eager to make his way in a new city. Like so many other young adults, he discovered hardship and disappointment.

item.title: Midsize Cities Held Steady as U.S. Population Growth Declined
item.description: New census estimates show that the nation’s largest cities saw the sharpest slowdowns in population growth over the past year amid declines in immigration and plunging birthrates.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Stacy Sodolak for The New York Times
item.media:description: Austin, Texas, grew to have one million residents, but overall, the growth of midsize cities outpaced that of the larger ones near them.

item.title: In Some Other Universe, This Might Be Funny
item.description: When MAHA turns into HAHA.
item.dc:creator: Frank Bruni and Bret Stephens

item.title: Why China Isn’t Worried A.I. Will Replace Its Workers
item.description: The difference in perspectives between superpowers is shaping the race for A.I. dominance.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Sophia Alvarez Boyd

item.title: Who’s Running in 2028?
item.description: We look at potential candidates for the nation’s top job.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Stephen Colbert Slams ‘Trump’s Fabulous Billionaire Boys Trip’
item.description: Late night hosts responded to President Trump’s arrival in Beijing on Wednesday, accompanied by 17 chief executives including Tim Cook and Elon Musk.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Prospect of Alex Murdaugh Retrial Puts Rural South Carolina Back in Spotlight
item.description: The State Supreme Court cited a local court clerk in reversing the result of one of the most-watched murder trials of the last decade. It has raised painful memories in the state’s Lowcountry region.
item.dc:creator: Shaila Dewan and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:description: Becky Hill, the former Colleton County clerk of court, during her guilty plea last year.

item.title: Conservative Influencers Tap a Nonprofit to Pay for Their Security
item.description: A charity is raising money to provide security, arguing that protecting some of right-wing media’s biggest stars is a public good.
item.dc:creator: Ken Bensinger
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nick Shirley, a right-wing YouTuber and influencer, left, at the Conservative Political Action Conference in Grapevine, Texas, in March.

item.title: Shakira, Madonna and BTS Are First World Cup Final Halftime Show
item.description: The previous World Cup final drew more than 500 million live viewers. This year’s matchup is scheduled for July 19 at MetLife Stadium.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:description: Shakira recorded the official World Cup song, “Dai Dai,” which features the Afrobeats star Burna Boy.
